title: The Missing Chemist
summary: An analytical chemist vanishes after a lecture. His assistant is missing, a Brighton trail looks planted, and a chemical cargo points east.
intro:
Thursday, 17 May 1894. Mrs Agnes Harcourt asks for help in finding her husband, Dr Edwin Harcourt, an analytical chemist of Craven Street. Harcourt was last seen on Tuesday night after a lecture at the Royal Institution. By Wednesday morning his laboratory had been found in disorder. His assistant, Silas Mallory, has not returned to his lodgings.

The police have no body, no ransom note, and no clear crime. Harcourt was preparing to secure a patent for a cheap hospital disinfectant, and several commercial men had shown interest in it.

Your task is to determine who caused Harcourt's disappearance, why, how it was done, and where Harcourt was taken.

visitRules.title: Harcourt's Laboratory
visitRules.text: The laboratory has been made to look ransacked, but the disorder is too selective. Costly instruments remain on the bench while one drawer has been forced after its papers were disturbed. Beneath the blotter lies a second-class Brighton ticket dated Tuesday. A tea cup gives off a bitter medicinal smell. A torn blue cargo label, showing only 'RUD...' and 'CAL...', is trapped under an overturned stool. The porter remembers a cab outside after half-past eight; the painted number may have been 317.

effects.addNotebook: Laboratory: staged disorder, Brighton ticket, bitter tea cup, blue cargo label, cab number 317.

visitRules.title: Harcourt's Laboratory (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The useful points remain the same: staged disorder, planted-looking ticket, bitter cup, blue cargo label, and cab number 317.

visitRules.title: Mrs Harcourt
visitRules.text: Mrs Harcourt insists Edwin would not run away. He expected to file a patent for a cheap hospital disinfectant before the week was out. She names his assistant, Silas Mallory of Greek Street, who had been nervous and short of money. She also recalls that Rudd & Vale tried to buy the formula outright and were refused.

effects.addNotebook: Mrs Harcourt names Mallory and Rudd & Vale; Harcourt was about to file the patent.

visitRules.title: Mrs Harcourt (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Mrs Harcourt can add little beyond her certainty that Edwin would not abandon his work voluntarily.

visitRules.title: The Royal Institution
visitRules.text: The lecture attendant remembers Harcourt leaving in good spirits. Mallory waited in the hall, pale and irritable. Nathaniel Rudd pressed Harcourt after the lecture and was rebuffed: 'I shall not sell what I have not yet secured.' Mallory heard the exchange.

effects.addNotebook: At the Royal Institution: Rudd wanted the formula; Harcourt refused; Mallory heard.

visitRules.title: The Royal Institution (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The attendant repeats that Rudd pressed Harcourt and that Mallory was listening.

visitRules.title: Mallory's Lodgings
visitRules.text: Mallory's room has been emptied in haste. A loose grate hides an IOU to Ezra Pike for £83, a pawn receipt for a silver watch initialled E.H., a clipping for the S.S. Mercia sailing to New York, and a North Star Assurance slip naming 'S. Merritt' beside Rudd & Vale cargo. The landlady says Mallory left before dawn with a carpet-bag.

effects.addNotebook: Mallory's room links debt, Harcourt's watch, S.S. Mercia, North Star Assurance, and the alias S. Merritt.

visitRules.title: Mallory's Lodgings (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Mallory's room remains the strongest link between the debt, the watch, the ship, and Rudd & Vale.

visitRules.title: Rudd & Vale
visitRules.text: Nathaniel Rudd is smooth and careful. He admits curiosity about Harcourt's formula but denies any improper dealing. The office smells of packing straw and carbolic spirit. A clerk enters with a blue crate label, then withdraws when he sees you; the colour matches the fragment from the laboratory.

effects.addNotebook: Rudd denies special dealing, but his office uses blue cargo labels like the laboratory fragment.

visitRules.title: Rudd & Vale (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Rudd remains smooth and unhelpful. The blue labels are still the crack in his denial.

visitRules.title: Peabody & Finch
visitRules.text: Mr Finch confirms that Harcourt intended to file a provisional specification by Friday. The papers name Harcourt alone as inventor. Mallory had delivered notes but had no claim in the draft. If the formula were sold before filing, priority could become difficult to prove.

effects.addNotebook: Patent papers: Harcourt alone would own the formula; Mallory had no claim.

visitRules.title: Peabody & Finch (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The solicitor's papers confirm motive: the formula was valuable, imminent, and not Mallory's.

visitRules.title: Ezra Pike
visitRules.text: Pike denies threatening anyone, then admits Mallory owed him £83 and had promised repayment before Friday. Mallory spoke of 'a passage out' and 'a gentleman who would pay well for a secret.' Pike did not know the gentleman's name and does not care to know it.

effects.addNotebook: Ezra Pike confirms Mallory's debt and says Mallory spoke of a passage out and a buyer for a secret.

visitRules.title: Ezra Pike (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Pike repeats that Mallory needed money by Friday and spoke of leaving.

visitRules.title: Bow Street Police Station
visitRules.text: The inspector has no body and no ransom note. He considers flight, kidnapping, and commercial misadventure all possible. He writes down Mallory's name when you mention the assistant's absence, but he wants a route and a place before acting.

effects.addNotebook: Police have no body or ransom note. They need a route and a place.

visitRules.title: Bow Street Police Station (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The inspector still wants concrete route and place information, not theories.

visitRules.title: The Blue Anchor
visitRules.text: The Blue Anchor offers three contradictory rumours. One sailor swears a chemist ran willingly to sea; another says Rudd & Vale are moving ordinary disinfectant; a third insists every missing Londoner turns up in America. The only useful point is that Rudd & Vale night carts have lately been seen nearer Limehouse than Wapping proper.

effects.addNotebook: Blue Anchor gossip is unreliable, but Rudd & Vale carts have been seen nearer Limehouse.

visitRules.title: The Blue Anchor (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The pub has more confidence than knowledge. The Limehouse rumour may still matter.

visitRules.title: The Evening Chronicle Office
visitRules.text: The clerk confirms that a young man matching Mallory bought several copies of today's paper before noon and asked whether shipping advertisements were kept in the front office. The office has no private clue beyond the printed issue.

effects.addNotebook: Mallory was interested in today's shipping advertisements.

visitRules.title: The Evening Chronicle Office (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The Chronicle adds no secret article; the printed paper is the useful source.

visitRules.title: St Bartholomew's Hospital
visitRules.text: A physician remembers Harcourt's disinfectant demonstration with enthusiasm. The hospital hoped to test it, but had not financed it. This weakens the idea of a hospital dispute while confirming the formula's real commercial value.

effects.addNotebook: Hospital interest confirms the formula's value, but provides no culprit.

visitRules.title: St Bartholomew's Hospital (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The hospital remains an interested observer, not a likely conspirator.

visitRules.title: Lady Ashcombe
visitRules.text: Lady Ashcombe is eager to suggest scandal. She disliked Rudd and found Mallory common, but knows nothing solid. Her useful recollection is that Harcourt was cheerful after the lecture and spoke of filing his patent 'before another rogue gets wind of it.'

effects.addNotebook: Lady Ashcombe confirms Harcourt meant to file the patent soon and did not seem ready to flee.

visitRules.title: Lady Ashcombe (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Lady Ashcombe's gossip grows more colourful but no more reliable.

visitRules.title: Railway Clerk
visitRules.text: The clerk checks Tuesday's ledger. The Brighton ticket was sold at 7:15 p.m. to a young man with ink-stained cuffs, not to a gentleman of Harcourt's age. The barrier record shows no matching ticket taken from Harcourt. 'Bought to be found, perhaps,' the clerk says.

effects.addNotebook: Railway clerk: the Brighton ticket was likely planted by someone matching Mallory.

visitRules.title: Railway Clerk (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The railway ledger still points to a false Brighton trail.

visitRules.title: Dr North, Analytical Chemist
visitRules.text: Dr North hears your description of the bitter cup and performs a quick comparison with common sedatives. Chloral hydrate would fit the odour and the effect: enough to stupefy a man and make him appear drunk or ill, not necessarily enough to kill him.

effects.addNotebook: Dr North: Harcourt was likely drugged with chloral hydrate and moved while helpless.

visitRules.title: Dr North, Analytical Chemist (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Dr North's conclusion remains: chloral hydrate, sufficient to render Harcourt helpless.

visitRules.title: Central Cab Registry
visitRules.text: Cab 317 belongs to Thomas Smee. He remembers a young man near Craven Street at about twenty minutes to nine, helping a heavy, half-conscious older gentleman into the cab and calling him an overcome uncle. They drove east to Caledonia Wharf, Limehouse.

effects.addNotebook: Cab 317 carried a young man and stupefied older gentleman from Craven Street to Caledonia Wharf.

visitRules.title: Central Cab Registry (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The cab trail runs from Craven Street to Caledonia Wharf.

visitRules.title: Henley Pawn & Pledge
visitRules.text: Henley identifies the receipt from your description. The watch was pledged at 10:20 on Tuesday by Silas Mallory, whom he knows by sight. The watch bore the initials E.H.; Mallory's hands shook badly enough for Henley to remember him.

effects.addNotebook: Henley: Mallory pawned Harcourt's watch at 10:20 p.m. Tuesday.

visitRules.title: Henley Pawn & Pledge (Repeat)
visitRules.text: Henley remains certain: Mallory pledged the E.H. watch late on Tuesday.

visitRules.title: North Star Assurance
visitRules.text: The assurance clerk checks the policy number from Mallory's room. It covers one crate of laboratory glass and chemical samples, insured by Rudd & Vale for New York under the name S. Merritt. The crate is listed for temporary storage at Caledonia Warehouse.

effects.addNotebook: North Star links S. Merritt, Rudd & Vale cargo, New York, and Caledonia Warehouse.

visitRules.title: North Star Assurance (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The cargo policy still links S. Merritt, Rudd & Vale, and Caledonia Warehouse.

visitRules.title: Port Records Office
visitRules.text: With the Mercia clipping, blue label, or insurance slip as context, the dock clerk searches the manifest. He finds Rudd & Vale cargo under S. Merritt, marked for Caledonia Warehouse before transfer to the S.S. Mercia before nine tonight. A margin note says: 'Do not open. Private chemical samples.'

effects.addNotebook: Port manifest: Rudd & Vale cargo under S. Merritt is at Caledonia Warehouse before sailing.

visitRules.title: Port Records Office (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The manifest points to Caledonia Warehouse and the S.S. Mercia.

visitRules.title: Caledonia Warehouse
visitRules.text: Caledonia Warehouse is quiet except for a frightened night watchman. In an upper storeroom, behind crates marked as laboratory glass, Edwin Harcourt is alive but weak. His notebook is half-copied on the table. Mallory's carpet-bag lies beside a trunk labelled S. Merritt. Rudd's blue labels are stacked by the door. Harcourt remembers tea in the laboratory, then waking here while Mallory demanded the final cipher for the formula.

effects.addNotebook: Caledonia Warehouse: Harcourt alive; Mallory alias/trunk; Rudd labels; formula theft confirmed.

visitRules.title: Caledonia Warehouse (Repeat)
visitRules.text: The warehouse has yielded the truth: Harcourt was drugged, carried here, and held until the formula could be copied and shipped.

genericLeadRules.text: The railway clerk explains that ledgers are exacting, but he needs a ticket, date, destination, or passenger name before he can help.
The analyst can help only with a clear substance, vessel, stain, or account of symptoms.
The insurance clerk cannot search the books without a policy number, insured name, or particular claim.
The dock clerk handles hundreds of crates a day. He needs a ship, warehouse, cargo mark, policy number, or consignee.
The registry can trace a cab by number, driver, or unusually precise journey, but not by rumour alone.
The pawnbroker keeps his books, but he cannot identify a pledge without a receipt, object, initials, or date.
The publican has plenty of ordinary gossip, but nothing that appears to touch the present matter.
You make enquiries, but nothing here appears to advance the present case.

solution.whoText: Silas Mallory, Harcourt's assistant. Nathaniel Rudd helped create the commercial escape route.
solution.whyText: Mallory needed money and knew Harcourt was about to file the patent without him. Rudd & Vale were willing to profit from the formula before ownership was secure.
solution.howText: Mallory drugged Harcourt's tea with chloral hydrate, staged a false Brighton departure, and moved Harcourt by cab while he appeared drunk or ill.
solution.whereText: Harcourt was taken to Caledonia Warehouse, Limehouse, where Rudd & Vale cargo was being prepared for the S.S. Mercia.
solution.explanation: Mallory created a false flight to Brighton while actually moving Harcourt east to Limehouse. The laboratory points to staging, the railway office exposes the ticket as a plant, Dr North explains the drugging, the cab registry proves the route, and the insurance and port records connect Rudd & Vale cargo to Caledonia Warehouse. Harcourt is recovered alive before Mallory and Rudd can finish copying the formula and move the cargo to the S.S. Mercia.

solutionQuestions.prompt: Why was the Brighton ticket planted?
solutionQuestions.modelAnswer: It was meant to make Harcourt look as if he left London voluntarily, while the real movement went east to Limehouse.
The debt gave Mallory urgent motive to sell the formula before Harcourt filed the patent.
It connects the laboratory on Tuesday night to Caledonia Wharf and explains how a drugged Harcourt was moved.
Rudd was the commercial buyer/accessory whose cargo arrangements and blue labels tied the stolen formula to Caledonia Warehouse.
Mallory still needed Harcourt to reveal or confirm the final details of the formula before shipping it away.

authorNotes.implementationWarning: Visit-driven playable draft. Evidence and fact tracking have been intentionally removed.
